nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rivers, tributaries, oxbows, and floodplain lakes; shaded areas with leaf litter, driftwood, and overhanging vegetation

decorPreferences: dense planting with Echinodorus, Vallisneria, and floating plants

diet.notes: Omnivorous with a bias toward plant matter and small invertebrates. Wild diet includes insects, larvae, terrestrial fruits, seeds, algae, and reportedly fish scales. In the aquarium, offer sinking algae wafers and spirulina-based flakes as staples, supplemented with live or frozen Daphnia, Artemia, Moina, and bloodworm. Blanched vegetables (spinach, zucchini, cucumber) and small fruit pieces are accepted. Ensure sinking foods reach the fish given its characteristic downward-angled posture.

goodWith: small to medium tetras (e.g., rummy nose, cardinal, neon)
avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving species that outcompete for food
Anostomus anostomus (Striped Headstander) — that species is aggressive and a plant-eater; the two are often confused but require very different tankmates

imageCandidates.notes: Clear lateral profile showing characteristic head-down posture and spotted pattern; attributed to Leandro Sousa.
Rio Negro specimen; shows natural blackwater coloration.
Essequibo (Guyana) specimen; useful for geographic comparison.

summary: The Spotted Headstander is a peaceful, mid-sized Amazonian characin from family Chilodontidae, best known for its 45° head-down swimming posture — a convergent trait with Anostomidae headstanders. Unlike the aggressive Striped Headstander (Anostomus anostomus), this species is genuinely peaceful and plant-safe. Requires soft, acidic blackwater conditions and a biologically mature aquarium.
careNotes:
The Spotted Headstander (Chilodus punctatus) belongs to family Chilodontidae, which is now treated as separate from Anostomidae, though both families convergently evolved the distinctive head-down resting and swimming posture at approximately 45°. This species should not be confused with the Striped Headstander (Anostomus anostomus, family Anostomidae), which is aggressive, a confirmed plant-eater, and incompatible with many community fish — C. punctatus is peaceful and plant-safe.

Keep in a biologically mature aquarium of at least 81 L with a 90 cm footprint, filtered to turn over the tank volume 4–5 times per hour. Soft, acidic water is important: target pH 5.5–7.0, gH 1–8, temperature 22–26°C for long-term health. Tannin-stained blackwater (achieved with Indian almond/catappa leaves or driftwood) is highly beneficial and reflects the rio Negro and Essequibo biotopes from which most specimens originate.

Maintain in groups of five or more — smaller groups cause shyness and low-level squabbling. Provided the tank has sufficient cover (dense planting, driftwood, leaf litter, subdued lighting), any intraspecific spats rarely result in physical damage. A dark substrate and low ambient light will bring out the best coloration and reduce stress.

Ensure sinking foods — algae wafers, spirulina pellets, catappa-leaf biofilm — are used, as the head-down feeding angle means the fish rarely feeds at the surface. Supplement with frozen Daphnia, Artemia, and Moina 2–3 times per week. Blanched vegetables are accepted.

New specimens may take a few days to settle and begin feeding; dim the tank and offer live Daphnia to encourage reluctant feeders. The species is sensitive to dissolved organic waste — pristine water quality and regular partial water changes are essential. See also: [striped-headstander] (Anostomus anostomus) — superficially similar posture but completely different family, temperament, and care requirements.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely documented. Presumably an egg-scatterer with no parental care, likely triggered by simulating the onset of the wet season — increasing soft, warm, acidic water and offering live foods. Specific protocols are not established in hobbyist literature.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Chilodus punctatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided taxonomy (Chilodontidae, Characiformes), native range (Ecuador, Peru, Brazil, Colombia, Guyana, Suriname), water parameters (temp 20–28°C, pH 5.0–7.5, hardness 18–179 ppm ≈ gH 1–10), adult size (70–80 mm SL ≈ 8–10 cm TL), minimum tank (81 L, 90×30 cm base), diet (omnivore — insects, larvae, fruits, algae, vegetables, live/frozen), temperament (peaceful), grouping and conspecific aggression notes, biotope (blackwater with driftwood, leaf litter), and a note that captive breeding has occurred but details are unavailable.

additional.name: FishBase — Chilodus punctatus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Chilodontidae, Characiformes), native range countries (Brazil/Pará/Apeú, Guyana, Suriname, Venezuela/western Orinoco), water parameters (temp 24–28°C, pH 6.0–7.0, hardness up to 10 dH), trophic level 2.8 (omnivore). FishBase max TL of 18 cm appears to be an outlier or data entry error inconsistent with SeriouslyFish (70–80 mm SL) and hobbyist consensus of 8–10 cm; 10 cm used as maximum.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Chilodus punctatus
additional.notes: Confirmed 45° head-down swimming posture, adult size up to 7 cm, distribution (northeastern South America, Loreto/Peru), IUCN Least Concern (2023), nocturnal/shade-dwelling habits, Chilodontidae family placement. Described body coloration (grayish-green with rows of brown specks, black longitudinal band, silver throat/belly).

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity; no reliable protocol is established. Presumably an egg-scatterer with no parental care. Simulating wet-season conditions (soft acidic water, increased temperature, heavy live-food feeding) is the recommended approach. Confirmed to have occurred in captivity but details are unpublished.
